fields.name: source
fields.label: Camera source (webcam index, RTSP/HTTP URL, or file path)

data.label: Capture live camera (cv2 → MJPEG)
data.description: Opens a live source with `cv2.VideoCapture` — `start.source` is whatever it accepts: a webcam index ("0"), an RTSP/HTTP camera URL, or a local video file path. This is the source-agnostic thesis made concrete: the SAME downstream detector runs against a laptop webcam, an IP camera, or (later) a Gazebo/Isaac simulated robot camera; only this string changes. Each captured frame is downscaled to ≤640px wide, JPEG-encoded, and written as ONE self-contained binary chunk over the Data/Out channel `frames`. The channel's transport is `nats-latest` (lossy-latest core NATS, NOT durable JetStream): if the detector falls behind, OLD frames drop whole and the latest wins — exactly camera semantics, and lossless per frame because each JPEG is independently decodable (unlike a dropped fragmented-MP4 fragment, which would corrupt the stream). A workflow run is bounded, so it captures a `duration_s` window at a target `fps`. LIVE-ONLY for a webcam: macOS gates camera access behind a TCC prompt — grant it, or point `source` at an RTSP/HTTP URL or a file path.

data.label: Detect + annotate (YOLO26 + ByteTrack)
data.description: Drains the LIVE `frames` channel and runs YOLO26 + ByteTrack per frame AS each arrives (`model.track(frame, persist=True)` keeps stable ids across the per-frame calls). Two outputs at once: (1) the Control/Out channel `detections` — one `{frame,label,confidence,bbox,track_id}` token per recognized object, emitted the moment its frame is processed (open → item* → close), folded by the `summary` step's `join: gather`; and (2) the Data/Out channel `annotated` (transport `jetstream` — durable, lossless, ordered, replayable) — the SAME frame with YOLO's boxes/labels/track-ids drawn on it (`res.plot()`), JPEG-encoded and written as one self-contained chunk. The `annotated` channel has TWO consumers off the one durable datastream: the instance view taps it and plays it as a live MJPEG feed (the frontend `mjpeg` render adapter splits the byte stream on JPEG EOI markers), AND the downstream `recorder` step drains it into an MP4 it registers in the file catalogue. So you WATCH the processed video as the run goes AND keep a durable recording — from a single channel, no WebRTC. Pure-cv2 (no PyAV) to avoid the libav clash. LIVE-ONLY: the first run pip-installs ultralytics (PyTorch + OpenCV) and downloads yolo26n.pt (~5 MB), past the 60 s test cap on a cold run; warm runs are fast.

data.label: Summarize detections
data.description: Consumes the detector's Control channel `detections` with `join: gather`, so the gather barrier re-orders the emitted detection tokens by index and parks the whole collection as `{ output: [<detection>, ...] }` — fed in as this step's input token. Reads the list off `input.output` and tallies the total object count, a per-class histogram (person/car/truck/bus/…), the number of distinct tracked objects (ByteTrack ids), and the peak per-frame count.

data.label: Record annotated feed → file catalogue
data.description: Drains the detector's durable `annotated` Data/In channel (transport `jetstream`: lossless, ordered, replayable — the right contract for a recorder) and assembles the annotated JPEG frames into a single MP4 with `cv2.VideoWriter` (mp4v), sized lazily to the first frame. When the stream closes it finalizes the file and registers it in the platform's file catalogue via the SDK `log_artifact(..., blocking=True)` — blocking so the upload + catalogue registration complete before this node (and therefore the run) finishes. The same `annotated` datastream also feeds the live MJPEG view in the instance panel; JetStream's Limits retention lets the browser tap and this in-net consumer read it independently. LIVE-ONLY: rides the same heavy detector run.

data.label: Await report + recording
data.description: AND-join (`mode: "all"`): waits for BOTH the `summary` report branch AND the `recorder` artifact branch before letting control reach End. This barrier is load-bearing — without it whichever branch finished first would fire End, the engine would declare the instance complete and tear the net down, and an in-flight recorder could be killed before its `log_artifact` upload finished. Gating End on both makes the persisted recording a guaranteed side effect of the run.
